question_text: A diver at a depth of 20 m experiences a water pressure of 200,000 Pa (excluding atmospheric pressure). Another diver at a depth of 40 m is twice as deep. Which of the following is true about the pressure experienced by the second diver?

options.label: A
options.text: The pressure will be 100,000 Pa because pressure decreases with depth.

options.label: B
options.text: The pressure will be approximately 400,000 Pa because pressure is directly proportional to depth.

options.label: C
options.text: The pressure will be 200,000 Pa because the depth is the same for both divers.

options.label: D
options.text: The pressure will be 800,000 Pa because pressure increases with the square of the depth.

explanation: Since pressure in a liquid is directly proportional to depth (P = ρgh), when depth doubles from 20 m to 40 m, pressure also doubles from 200,000 Pa to approximately 400,000 Pa. This tests whether students understand the linear relationship between depth and pressure, not a squared or inverse relationship. Distractors test common misconceptions about how pressure varies with depth.
